items.q: What attitude does Pop Art take toward consumer culture?
items.correct: Ambiguous - it both celebrates and critiques mass media imagery.
items.why: Students read Pop Art as either pure celebration or pure protest. Warhol's deadpan repetition of Marilyn or soup cans is deliberately AMBIGUOUS, leaving viewers to decide if it's homage or critique.

items.q: Why is preserving white in watercolour important?
items.correct: White comes from the paper showing through - there is no true white pigment in traditional watercolour.
items.misconception: You can simply paint white over other colours later.
items.random_wrong: Because white is the most expensive colour.
items.why: Students plan to add white at the end as in opaque media. Watercolour is transparent - highlights must be PRESERVED from the start by leaving paper untouched or using masking fluid.

items.q: What is the rule of thirds?
items.correct: A guideline placing key elements along lines or intersections of a 3x3 grid.
items.misconception: A strict mathematical rule that must never be broken.
items.why: Students treat it as inviolable. It's a useful HEURISTIC for engaging compositions - many great works ignore it. Centred compositions can be powerful too; the rule is a starting point, not a law.
The answer examiners want is: Oxidation. “Evaporation” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A traditional white priming layer is called:
items.why: Correct answer: Gesso. “Glaze” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: An artist applies 4 oil glazes per panel and produces 9 panels for a series. How many glazes in total?
items.why: The real figure is 36. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
